era_context: Late 14th century: Ming Dynasty consolidates control in China; Ottoman Empire expands in the Balkans; Western Europe experiences the Great Schism; the Timurid Renaissance develops in Central Asia; the Joseon Dynasty rules Korea; the Aztec Empire continues its expansion in Mesoamerica.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond major chronicle centers

events.title: Portuguese Expedition to Morocco (Ceuta Campaign preparations)
events.description: Portuguese forces, under the direction of Prince Henry the Navigator and King John I, began preparations and reconnaissance for the conquest of Ceuta, a Moroccan fortress city. The actual conquest occurred in 1415, but 1383 marks the beginning of strategic planning and naval expeditions that would characterize Portuguese expansion into North Africa and eventually the Atlantic. These early campaigns were motivated by crusading ideology and economic interest in controlling trade routes.
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (1394–1460, though young in 1383)

sources.name: Crónica de D. João I (Chronicle of King John I, by Fernão Lopes, written early 15th century)
Portuguese royal records and later historiography

events.certainty_note: The exact date of Portuguese planning for North African expansion is uncertain. 1383 is assigned as a probable year for early reconnaissance, but the campaign itself is not documented as occurring in this specific year.

events.title: Earthquake in China (Shaanxi Province)
events.description: A significant earthquake struck Shaanxi Province in central China, causing considerable damage and loss of life. The earthquake was recorded in Ming Dynasty records and is noted in seismic histories of China. Such natural disasters were often interpreted as signs of heaven's displeasure with the ruling dynasty, though the Ming Dynasty's legitimacy was not seriously threatened by this event.

sources.name: Chinese seismic historical records and modern seismological compilations

events.certainty_note: The earthquake is recorded in Ming official records, which provide contemporary documentation. However, precise magnitude and casualty figures are not reliably preserved.

events.title: Ottoman Siege of Nish (Niš)
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murat I besieged and captured the Serbian fortress city of Niš (Nish), a major stronghold in the Balkans. The capture of Niš was a significant step in Ottoman consolidation of control over the Serbian lands and marked the decline of Serbian independence. The city became an important Ottoman administrative and military center. This conquest was part of the broader Ottoman expansion that would culminate in the Battle of Kosovo in 1389.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman conquest of Niš is traditionally dated to 1383, though some sources place it in 1382 or 1384. The date 1383 is accepted by most modern Ottoman historians, but uncertainty of ±1 year is appropriate.

events.title: Tamerlane's Conquest of Isfāhān
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane) conquered the Persian city of Isfāhān (Isfahan), one of the major cultural and economic centers of the Timurid world. The conquest was marked by significant destruction and loss of life, though later sources may have exaggerated the scale of devastation. Isfāhān became an important center of the Timurid Renaissance, with patronage of arts, architecture, and scholarship. The conquest consolidated Timur's control over Persia.

sources.name: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi, Zafarnama (biography of Timur, written early 15th century)
Ibn Arabshah, Aja'ib al-Maqdur (account of Timur's life)

events.certainty_note: Timur's conquest of Isfāhān is documented in later biographical sources. The date 1383 is accepted by most modern historians, though some sources place it slightly earlier or later (±1–2 years).

events.title: Wycliffe's Final Years and Continued Influence
events.description: John Wycliffe, the English theologian and reformer, was in his final year of life (he died in late 1384 or 1383, depending on the source). In 1383, he continued to advocate for church reform and the translation of the Bible into English, despite increasing opposition from church authorities. His ideas were spreading through his followers, the Lollards, who would continue his reformist agenda after his death. The year 1383 marks the end of his active ministry.

sources.name: Knighton's Chronicle (Henry Knighton, contemporary English chronicler)
Wycliffe's own writings and later biographical compilations

events.certainty_note: Wycliffe's death is traditionally dated to 31 December 1384, but some sources place it in late 1383. This entry reflects the uncertainty in the exact date.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Ottoman conquests in the Balkans (Niš, Sofia) is uncertain, with sources varying by ±1–2 years. Some modern scholars question whether the scale of destruction attributed to Timur's conquest of Isfāhān in later sources is historically accurate, suggesting that later chroniclers may have exaggerated the devastation. Wycliffe's death date is disputed between 1383 and 1384, reflecting uncertainty in contemporary records. The Portuguese expedition to Morocco in 1383 is speculative and based on later historiography rather than contemporary documentation.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1383 is moderate but regionally uneven. European sources (English, Ottoman, Portuguese) are relatively abundant, though Ottoman records are often later compilations. Chinese sources are rich but sometimes lack precise dating. Middle Eastern sources are fragmentary and often written decades after the events. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Oceania are essentially undocumented. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Ottoman and Timurid sources may reflect court biases and victor narratives.

graph_edges.note: Ottoman conquest of Niš was followed by the conquest of Sofia in 1384, part of a coordinated campaign.
Timur's conquest of Isfāhān in 1383 was part of his broader Middle Eastern campaigns.
Wycliffe's final years of ministry in 1383 led to his death in 1384 and the continuation of his reformist legacy.
